items.subject: architecture
items.topic: how the skyscraper became possible with steel-frame construction
items.transcript: The skyscraper is often described as a triumph of ambition, but it's really a triumph of engineering. Before the 1880s, tall buildings had a hard ceiling, and the reason was the walls. Traditional buildings were load-bearing masonry, meaning the outer walls carried the entire weight of the structure. The taller you built, the thicker those walls had to be at the base to support everything above. A famous example is the Monadnock Building in Chicago, sixteen stories tall, with ground-floor walls nearly six feet thick. At that point the walls were eating the usable space, and the whole approach hit a practical limit. Then came a shift in thinking. Engineers developed the steel skeleton frame, in which an internal grid of steel columns and beams carries the load instead of the walls. Now the walls became just a skin, a curtain hung on the frame, letting in light and keeping out weather. The Home Insurance Building, completed in Chicago in 1885, is usually credited as the first to use this method. Once the structural burden moved to the steel frame, height was no longer limited by wall thickness. Of course, steel alone wasn't enough. A tall building is useless if people can't reach the upper floors, so the safety elevator was equally essential. It turns out the skyscraper wasn't a single invention at all. It was the convergence of several technologies arriving at the same moment, and together they let cities finally grow upward rather than only outward.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How steel-frame construction made tall skyscrapers possible
options.B: The history of building construction in the city of Chicago
options.C: Why load-bearing masonry walls eventually became fireproof
options.D: The design of elevators used in early modern buildings

questions.explanation: 正确答案A：讲座核心是钢框架结构如何取代承重墙、从而突破高度限制，使摩天大楼成为可能。B属于wrong_topic：芝加哥只是举例背景，不是讲座主题。C属于surface_word_trap：借用了load-bearing masonry关键词，但讲座从未讨论墙体防火。D属于too_narrow：电梯只是结尾一笔带过的辅助技术，不是主旨。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what limited the height of buildings before the 1880s?

options.A: The lack of reliable safety elevators for tall structures
options.B: Load-bearing walls that had to grow thicker at the base
options.C: A shortage of steel columns and beams for construction
options.D: City regulations that capped buildings at sixteen stories

questions.explanation: 正确答案B：讲座明确说传统承重墙必须在底部越建越厚才能支撑上方重量，这就是高度限制的原因。A属于topic_adjacent：电梯在此语境是使建筑可用的因素，但不是1880年代前限制高度的原因。C属于reversed_logic：钢材是后来的解决方案，不是当时短缺造成的限制。D属于wrong_detail：十六层是Monadnock大楼的具体例子，讲座并未提到任何法规上限。

questions.stem: What does the professor say about the walls in a steel-frame building?

options.A: They were removed entirely to let in more sunlight
options.B: They were reinforced to help the steel carry the load
options.C: They became a skin hung on the frame, not load-bearing
options.D: They were built nearly six feet thick at the base

questions.explanation: 正确答案C：讲座说钢框架承重后，墙变成挂在框架上的一层皮肤（curtain），只负责采光和挡风雨。A属于wrong_detail：墙并未被完全去除，而是变成幕墙。B属于reversed_logic：不是墙帮钢材承重，而是钢材承重、墙不再承重。D属于wrong_detail：六英尺厚是承重墙Monadnock大楼的旧做法，与钢框架的薄墙相反。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the invention of the skyscraper?

options.A: It was primarily the achievement of a single engineer
options.B: It would have been impossible in a city like Chicago
options.C: It mattered less than the invention of the safety elevator
options.D: It depended on several technologies arriving together

questions.explanation: 正确答案D：讲座结尾明确暗示摩天大楼不是单一发明，而是钢框架、安全电梯等多项技术在同一时刻汇聚的结果。A属于reversed_logic：讲座恰恰强调它不是单一发明或单人成就。B属于topic_adjacent：芝加哥其实是摩天大楼诞生地，与暗示相反。C属于over_generalization：讲座说电梯同样必要，但并未说它比钢框架更重要。

items.subject: social_psychology
items.transcript: Imagine you're a strong math student, and right before a hard test, someone reminds you of a stereotype that people in your group aren't good at math. Even if you don't believe it, that reminder can quietly hurt your score. This is a phenomenon social psychologists call stereotype threat. The basic idea is that when people are aware of a negative stereotype about a group they belong to, the fear of confirming that stereotype creates extra pressure. And that pressure uses up mental resources, resources you'd otherwise spend on the actual task. The classic study comes from Claude Steele and Joshua Aronson in the 1990s. They gave a difficult verbal test to Black and white college students. When the test was described as a genuine measure of intelligence, Black students scored lower. But when the very same test was described as just a problem-solving exercise, with no mention of ability, that gap shrank dramatically. Nothing about the students changed. Only the framing of the test changed. So what's happening in the mind? The worry itself is distracting. Part of your attention is spent monitoring how you're doing and dreading failure, which leaves less working memory for solving the problems in front of you. The encouraging part is that stereotype threat can be reduced. Simply reframing a task, or reminding students of their own values before a test, has been shown to narrow performance gaps. It's a reminder that performance isn't just about ability. The situation and the words around a task can shape how well people actually do.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why some groups are naturally better at math than others
options.B: How awareness of a stereotype can lower a person's performance
options.C: The design of intelligence tests used in college admissions
options.D: How working memory improves with regular academic practice

questions.explanation: 正确答案B：讲座主旨是意识到关于自身群体的负面刻板印象会带来压力，从而降低学业表现。A属于wrong_topic：讲座明确反对天生能力差异的说法。C属于surface_word_trap：借用intelligence test关键词，但讲座不谈测试设计或招生。D属于wrong_topic：working memory只是解释机制的一环，不是主旨，且方向也不对。

questions.stem: Why does the professor describe the Steele and Aronson study?

options.A: To argue that verbal tests are unfair to certain students
options.B: To suggest that intelligence tests should be banned
options.C: To provide evidence that test framing affects performance
options.D: To explain how the researchers created the difficult test

questions.explanation: 正确答案C：教授引用该研究是为了用实证证明——仅改变测试的描述方式就能改变成绩差距，从而支持刻板印象威胁的观点。A属于wrong_purpose：教授的目的不是控诉考试不公，而是展示框架效应。B属于over_inference：教授从未主张废除智力测试。D属于literal_restatement：只是复述研究内容（做了个难测试），没有解释为什么提到它。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why does stereotype threat hurt performance?

options.A: Because students stop believing they can do the task
options.B: Because the test itself becomes objectively harder
options.C: Because students lack preparation for difficult exams
options.D: Because worry uses up working memory needed for the task

questions.explanation: 正确答案D：讲座说担忧本身分散注意力，占用了本可用于解题的工作记忆。A属于wrong_detail：讲座甚至指出即使你不相信刻板印象也会受影响，重点不是丧失信念。B属于reversed_logic：测试难度并未改变，改变的只是描述方式。C属于topic_adjacent：准备不足是常见解释，但讲座并未把它作为原因。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about academic performance?

options.A: It can be shaped by the situation, not just by ability
options.B: It is fixed and cannot be changed once measured
options.C: It depends entirely on how a test is worded
options.D: It is unaffected by a student's personal values

questions.explanation: 正确答案A：讲座结尾暗示表现不仅取决于能力，情境和用词也会影响实际发挥。B属于reversed_logic：讲座恰恰表明表现可被改变（差距能缩小）。C属于over_generalization：措辞是重要因素，但说“完全取决于”过于绝对。D属于topic_adjacent：讲座提到提醒学生自身价值观能缩小差距，与该选项相反。

items.subject: art_history
items.topic: how Japanese woodblock prints influenced European modern art
items.transcript: When Japan reopened to trade in the mid-nineteenth century, something unexpected traveled to Europe alongside the tea and porcelain: Japanese woodblock prints. These prints, known as ukiyo-e, arrived almost by accident, sometimes used as packing paper to cushion fragile goods. But European artists who saw them were captivated, and the fascination that followed became known as Japonisme. So what did these artists actually find so striking? For one, the prints used flat areas of bold, unmixed color, without the careful shading Western painting relied on to create the illusion of depth. They also featured bold, asymmetrical compositions, often cropping a figure or object right at the edge of the frame, as if the scene continued beyond it. And they treated everyday subjects, actors, travelers, workers, with the same seriousness Western art reserved for grand historical or religious scenes. You can see the impact directly. Vincent van Gogh collected hundreds of these prints and even copied several in oil paint. Edgar Degas borrowed those unusual cropped, off-center viewpoints for his images of dancers. Mary Cassatt adopted the flat color and clean outlines in her prints of mothers and children. The influence went deeper than borrowing a few motifs. Japanese prints helped European artists question the long-standing rule that a painting had to imitate a three-dimensional scene through perspective and shading. Instead, a picture could be a flat, deliberately arranged surface of shape and color. That idea helped open the door to the modern art that followed.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How Japan reopened its ports to European trade
options.B: The techniques used to make Japanese woodblock prints
options.C: How Japanese prints influenced European modern art
options.D: The life and paintings of Vincent van Gogh

questions.explanation: 正确答案C：讲座核心是日本浮世绘版画如何影响欧洲现代艺术。A属于wrong_topic：日本开港只是引出背景，不是主题。B属于wrong_topic：讲座谈版画的艺术特征与影响，而非制作工艺。D属于too_narrow：梵高只是几个例子之一，不是主旨。

questions.stem: According to the professor, how did the woodblock prints first reach Europe?

options.A: They were commissioned directly by European art collectors
options.B: They were sent as diplomatic gifts to European rulers
options.C: They were displayed at major international art exhibitions
options.D: They arrived by accident, sometimes as packing paper

questions.explanation: 正确答案D：讲座说这些版画几乎是偶然抵达欧洲，有时被当作包装纸缓冲易碎品。A属于topic_adjacent：欧洲收藏家后来确实喜爱它们，但最初并非受委托订制。B属于topic_adjacent：外交礼物是合理的历史猜测，但讲座未如此说。C属于wrong_detail：讲座未提及最初通过展览传入，反而强调是偶然。

questions.stem: What does the professor say Degas borrowed from the prints?

options.A: The cropped, off-center viewpoints for his dancers
options.B: The flat color and clean outlines for mothers and children
options.C: The habit of copying entire scenes in oil paint
options.D: The use of grand historical and religious subjects

questions.explanation: 正确答案A：讲座明确说Degas借用了那种不寻常的裁切、偏心构图来画舞者。B属于wrong_detail：平涂色彩和清晰轮廓是Cassatt采用的，不是Degas。C属于wrong_detail：临摹整幅画作的是van Gogh。D属于reversed_logic：宏大历史宗教题材正是西方旧传统，浮世绘反其道而行之，Degas并未借用它。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the deeper influence of Japanese prints?

options.A: They convinced Europeans to abandon painting entirely
options.B: They challenged the idea that a painting must imitate depth
options.C: They proved Western perspective techniques were incorrect
options.D: They mainly affected the choice of subjects, not style

questions.explanation: 正确答案B：讲座暗示日本版画促使欧洲艺术家质疑“绘画必须通过透视和明暗模仿三维场景”这一长期规则。A属于over_generalization：讲座说他们重新思考绘画方式，而非放弃绘画。C属于reversed_logic：讲座并未说透视技法是错的，只是说它不是唯一方式。D属于topic_adjacent：讲座明确说影响不止于题材，还深入到风格与观念。

items.subject: history
items.topic: the unexpected origins of the modern fire department
items.transcript: When you picture a fire department, you probably imagine a public service, funded by taxes and open to everyone. But that's a fairly modern arrangement. For a long stretch of history, firefighting was actually a private business, and a surprisingly cutthroat one. Consider early eighteenth-century London and, later, American cities. Fire protection was tied to insurance companies. If you insured your building, the company gave you a metal plaque, called a fire mark, to nail to your wall. Each insurer ran its own brigade of firefighters. Now here's the catch. When a fire broke out, brigades would rush to the scene and check for their company's fire mark. If the burning building carried a rival company's mark, or no mark at all, they might simply stand back and let it burn. In some cities, competing brigades reportedly fought each other in the street over who had the right to put out a blaze, while the building was consumed behind them. This system was obviously inefficient and dangerous. A fire that spread from an uninsured house could easily reach an insured one next door. Over time, cities recognized that fire was a shared threat that ignored property lines, and that protecting the whole community was more sensible than protecting individual customers. Gradually, private brigades were replaced by unified, publicly funded fire departments that responded to every fire regardless of who owned the building. So the fire truck that comes for anyone today grew out of a system that once came only for paying clients.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How insurance companies calculate the risk of fires
options.B: The dangers of living in eighteenth-century London
options.C: How metal fire marks were designed and manufactured
options.D: How firefighting changed from a private business to a public service

questions.explanation: 正确答案D：讲座主旨是消防如何从私营保险生意演变为面向所有人的公共服务。A属于wrong_topic：讲座不谈保险公司如何计算风险。B属于too_narrow：伦敦只是举例，且危险性不是主题。C属于surface_word_trap：借用fire marks关键词，但讲座不谈其设计制造。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention brigades checking for a fire mark?

options.A: To show that early firefighting served only paying clients
options.B: To explain how metal plaques were attached to walls
options.C: To praise the efficiency of privately run fire brigades
options.D: To describe how insurance companies advertised themselves

questions.explanation: 正确答案A：教授提到消防队查看fire mark，是为了说明早期消防只服务于投保的付费客户。B属于literal_restatement：只是复述钉牌子这个动作，没解释目的。C属于wrong_purpose：讲座恰恰说该系统低效危险，并非赞美其效率。D属于over_inference：把广告宣传这一未表达的意图强加给教授。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what might a brigade do at a building with a rival's mark?

options.A: Put out the fire and later bill the rival company
options.B: Stand back and let the building burn
options.C: Nail their own company's fire mark over the rival's
options.D: Call the city's public fire department for help

questions.explanation: 正确答案B：讲座明确说若着火建筑挂着对手公司的标志或没有标志，消防队可能袖手旁观、任其燃烧。A属于topic_adjacent：向对手收费听起来合理，但讲座未提。C属于wrong_detail：讲座说的是查看标志决定是否灭火，没有覆盖对手标志的说法。D属于reversed_logic：当时还不存在统一的公共消防部门，那是后来才出现的。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply led cities to create public fire departments?

options.A: Insurance companies voluntarily merged their private brigades
options.B: Firefighters demanded higher wages from the government
options.C: Fire threatened the whole community, not just paying customers
options.D: Metal fire marks became too expensive to produce

questions.explanation: 正确答案C：讲座暗示城市意识到火灾是无视产权界线的共同威胁，保护整个社区比只保护付费客户更明智，这促成了公共消防。A属于topic_adjacent：合并私营队伍是结果的一部分，但讲座说是城市认识到威胁而推动，非保险公司自愿。B属于topic_adjacent：工资问题从未提及。D属于wrong_detail：讲座从未说标志成本过高是原因。
